data.title: Alphabet's $2T Catapult: Sum Its Assets And Get Google Search For Free
data.description:
This is an article I have long been thinking about and researching. You could consider my previous article a prequel which was published in December under the title "If Robotaxi Is The Next Frontier, Either Tesla Or Google Is Completely Mispriced". Here, I considered the nascent robotaxi market and discussed an idea. In Tesla's case, robotaxis are a big reason - if not, the primary reason - that many investors use to justify its valuation. But Waymo, which, in contrast, already runs robotaxi trips in several cities, doesn't seem to benefit from the same multiples investors are awarding to Tesla. So, if Tesla's investors are right, Google is meaningfully undervalued; conversely, if Waymo's valuation is correct, then Tesla's investors will see their stock re-rated with lower multiples.

Now, I want to expand on this idea to explain why Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG; NASDAQ:GOOGL) is significantly undervalued and could offer a compelling opportunity right before our eyes, while we are in the midst of a strange bull market where, although investors are cautious, stocks continue to move up in true bull mode.

So, we will talk about Google, and I will explain what I stated in the title. In short, I believe Google currently represents one of the most convincing opportunities in a very demanding market, where investor enthusiasm for AI is challenged daily by a harsher geopolitical environment, with the risk of wars (commercial or military, that they may be) continually increasing. Incidentally, we should not forget that peace is the best condition for economies to prosper.

Let's get back to the thesis. I will walk you through a sum-of-the-parts analysis that compares Google's different assets to their respective peers. To do so, I will use Google's FY2024 results and, where possible, the FY2025 estimates.

Google's Assets

First of all, let's take a look at how Google breaks down its revenues. We have Google Services, Google Cloud, and Other Bets. Google Services comprises Google Ads, Android, Chrome, Search, and YouTube. This business mainly makes money through advertising and marketing, but consumer subscriptions are becoming more and more meaningful for YouTube. Google Cloud is the cloud business and makes money from consumption-based and subscription fees for the cloud services Google offers (infrastructure, platform, applications, etc.). Other Bets is a far smaller business unit, inside which we can find many new endeavours that Google calls "moonshots". Well, one of these is none other than Waymo.

As we can read below, Google Services grossed $305B, Google Cloud did $43.2B, and Other Bets reported $1.6B. When we look at the operating income of these three segments, we see that Google Services reached $121.3B, Google Cloud $6.1B, while Other Bets was unprofitable and reported a loss of $4.4B. Considering that Alphabet-level activities (the parent company's projects that don't fall under any of these three categories, which mainly means AI-related research projects), we see that it is Google Services that roughly pays the bill for the rest of the company, with Google Cloud contributing with $6B in operating income. However, if we read what happened from 2022 to 2024 (by the way, I appreciate it a lot when annual reports include at least three years instead of only two), we see that Google Cloud is rapidly scaling and turning to a very profitable business: three years ago, it lost almost $2B, while now it is up $8B from that level and rapidly growing. Google Services has not been idle either, and it has increased its operating income by almost 50% from 2022 to 2024. Google Services thus has an operating margin close to 40%, while Google Cloud is at 14%. But when we consider that Google Cloud is catching up with Amazon AWS and Microsoft Azure, whose margins usually hover between 37% and 40%, we should expect Google Cloud to report strong growth. As a matter of fact, at the end of Q1 2025, Alphabet reported an operating margin growth of 230 bps to 33.9%, with Cloud reaching a 17.8% operating margin (Q1 2024: 9.4%). So, if you are looking for a story of strong and predictable growth at high rates, Google Cloud now seems the place to be.

Let's continue our understanding of Google's revenues. We see below that Google Search accounts for $198B of revenue, which means that it is worth around 65% of Google Services' revenues, while YouTube ads reached $36.1B. Google Network (the private network infrastructure that underlies Google's products) reported $30.4B. The sum of these three contributes to Google advertising, which reached $265B in revenues at the end of last year. The remaining $40B come from subscriptions.

So, given these numbers, my thesis could already seem to be thrown off.

But let's now turn to what Mr. Market is doing.

Right now, we could say that Google's valuation is mainly linked to its advertising business. Well, the most similar business that is valued as an advertising champion is Meta (META). Right now, Mark Zuckerberg's company trades at a fwd earnings multiple of 27.7x, while Google trades below the market average at a 19. This is not without reason. Overall, Meta's profitability is higher, with a gross margin of 82%, an EBIT margin of 42% and a net income margin of 39%, while Google has a gross margin of 59%, an EBIT margin of 33% and a net income margin of 31%. However, I think that the gap between a 19 and a 27.7 earnings multiple is too wide when compared to these profitability metrics. Meta's growth is currently a bit faster, with an expected EBIT growth of 21.1% versus Google's 19.2%. But while Meta seems fairly priced, I would think that Google's advertising business could well be valued at a 24x earnings multiple. What does this imply? Simply put, Google's advertising business could make around $110B in net income at the end of this year. Using the 24x multiple, we have a valuation of $2.6T, almost 20% above Google's current market cap. This means we are getting everything else for free, plus a decent upside on Google's ad business.

But the narrative around Google doesn't allow us to simply stop here and be content. As soon as we support the thesis that Google, as it is right now, is deeply undervalued, we need to fend off a plethora of people arguing that Google Search (and its ad business) is dead because Google lost the AI race as ChatGPT is stealing market share. You will find charts such as the one below, showing OpenAI's dominance in gen AI traffic.

So, let's give Google Search for dead. Let's simply wipe it out of our equation and focus on Google's two remaining assets: Google Cloud and Other Bets. Within Other Bets, let's consider only Waymo and not all of the other projects Alphabet is investing in.

Alphabet Without Search

Let's take away from Alphabet the Google Search business and the Google Network business, while we keep YouTube ads and Google subscriptions, platforms, and devices.

Google Cloud

Starting with Google Cloud, we see that it earned $6B in operating income. We know it is a fast-growing business in an industry that still sees a lot of expansion. Right now, Google Cloud is estimated to have a market share of 12%, trailing Azure's 21% and Amazon's 30%.

Now, when we consider Amazon, a big part of the investment thesis hinges on AWS. If Amazon reports slower AWS growth for one quarter, the stock plunges. If AWS speeds up, investors celebrate. Right now, Amazon's market cap is $2.4T. AWS crossed the threshold of $100B in revenues. With a net income margin of around 25%, we have $25B of Amazon's FY24 net income of $66B, coming from AWS. If we use a 30x multiple, we find that AWS is worth around $750B by itself. I would bet people would be willing to pay $1T to own this business. But let's stick to $750B. This is 30% of Amazon's current market cap.

Now, Google Cloud is growing at a faster pace, even though it is not the leading cloud operator. Moreover, it is quickly scaling and ramping up its profitability. So my expectation for the end of this year is to see an operating income of $11B. This makes me estimate that its net income could be close to $7B. I will use a 32x multiple, and we have that Google Cloud could be worth $225B. Again, I bet there will be plenty of people willing to pay much more to own this business. But part of Google Cloud's value is its integration within the Google ecosystem. As a standalone company, a value a bit north of $200B makes sense right now.

This means we have $2T of Alphabet's current market cap that still needs to be explained.

Google Subscriptions

Well, let's talk about Google subscriptions, which encompasses recurring-fee services such as YouTube Premium, YouTube Music, YouTube TV, Google Play Pass, and Google cloud storage plans for Drive and Gmail. It also comprises the digital platform fees and hardware device sales. It reported $40B in revenue. While Alphabet doesn't report the standalone operating income line for this business, we can use Apple's subscription business as a comparable peer, where we know the gross margin is 75%. This means the earnings margin could be above 50%. Well, Apple surely doesn't only trade according to the behavior and the results of its subscription services business (iPhone sales are still the core business, no matter how you look at it), but this business accounts for almost $100B in revenue and generates high profits. We could consider 35% of Apple's market cap as the value of its services business, which means Apple's services is worth around $1T. This means the earnings multiple is around 22. But Google's business also includes platforms and devices. In other words, it is a small version of what Apple is as a whole. Apple currently trades at a fwd PE of 29. Let's consider Apple worthy of a premium. We could, in any case, have Google trade at a multiple of 22x. The result is that Google subscriptions could have yearly earnings of around $18B-$22B and be valued at $440B.

So, we are at $660B.

YouTube

What about YouTube? Currently, Google One and YouTube have more than 200M subscribers. YouTube ads raked in $36B in revenue and is headed to cross the $40B threshold.

You have probably heard of the so-called streaming wars and how Netflix (NFLX) won. Well, while competitors such as Warner Media and Disney were crushed, there was a silent newcomer creeping in until it became the true elephant in the room: YouTube. We just read reports where YouTube and Netflix dominate U.S. TV viewing time, with YouTube almost doubling Netflix, with 12.5% of viewership time against the 7.5% of Netflix. YouTube has a weapon that Netflix can't count on: creators whose costs are extremely low. Netflix has to spend on content, YouTube doesn't. Moreover, YouTube video library is a key asset in Google's AI dominance in videomaking.

Now, Netflix has a market cap of $532B, and many believe Netflix could double it by 2030. If Netflix is worth $532B, how much is YouTube worth? Netflix has 300M subscribers, while YouTube and Google One top 200M. So Netflix still has a larger subscriber base. But YouTube is much more profitable: it has almost no content spending and pays its creators through ad revenue. So, even though we don't know the profitability metrics for YouTube, I would be willing to bet it has much higher margins than Netflix. Based on its market share and its cost structure, I would value YouTube, together with YouTube ads, at around $750B.

We are at $1.41T.

Waymo

And we finally reach Waymo. We will leave aside whether Waymo's technology is better than Tesla's (the debate on LIDAR vs. cameras fills any robotaxi discussion forum). We heard that Waymo recently achieved a post-money valuation of $45B, after the $5.6B series C round in October 2024. Waymo currently serves over 250k paid weekly rides and is rapidly expanding in new cities. However, the robotaxi hype still turns to Tesla (TSLA), whose market cap is $1T. Cathie Wood, for example, believes that this business alone will make up 90% of the target price of $2,600 for the stock that she believes Tesla will reach in five years. In other words, she believes robotaxis will be a $9T opportunity. While she may have an estimate that few of us agree with, we should still consider why Tesla has a market cap of $1T. I recently explained why I believe its value is linked to unpredictable variables, such as Elon Musk's behavior. I also explained how we could be so bold as to value Tesla's automotive business at $100B. Let's consider that Tesla's robotics business is worth around $250B, which is a very generous concession, as of right now. Well, we have around $650B, which could be linked to the robotaxi business. So far, Tesla has defied expectations and has continued trading at very high valuations even though many, including myself, argued that it was severely overvalued. If we believe the market is right about Tesla's case and the future of its arbitrator, then we have to explain why Waymo, which is considerably ahead of Tesla in running its robotaxi fleet, should be worth only $45B.

If we use the same metrics for Tesla and Waymo, we have Tesla's robotaxi business that is currently valued between $400B and $600B. I see no reason why Waymo should be worth less. So, I would say that, if Tesla's investors are correct, then Waymo should trade between $600B and $800B. Guess what the sum is?

We are right between $2T and $2.2T, which is Alphabet's current market cap.

Conclusion

This analysis is meant to be a bit provocative. Perhaps, financial markets are not as efficient as we think they are. But, no matter how we twist it, Google's valuation currently doesn't make sense. We are basically buying the stock and getting its core Search Business for free (which, by the way, is far from being a dead business). This is why Google is currently one of my most convincing picks, and I have made it the core holding of my growth portfolio.

data.title: Qualcomm And Its Real Value
data.description:
Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) manufactures wireless chipsets, licenses technology, and provides communication services around the world. Qualcomm delivers leading-edge AI, high-performance, lower-power computing, and the company’s mission aims for next-generation digital transformation.

QCOM CFO Akash Palkhiwala comments at the Nasdaq London Investor Conference have emphasized increased global automotive digitization.

“So, when you look at those things, the silicon that we participate in is growing north of 15%, 20% every year because of just an incredible amount of digitization happening in the car. And so Qualcomm is in a great position to take advantage of that change and really offer the technologies that are required in the car,” said Akash Palkhiwala.

Diversification efforts in automotive and edge AI are enabling new revenue streams and prospects for Qualcomm. The Snapdragon digital chassis, another technology product Qualcomm offers, has secured 30 new design wins, including five ADAS programs, and 14 new vehicles launches utilizing its platforms.

Additional technologies developed by Qualcomm in 2024 such as the Snapdragon 8 Elite are poised to lead as the world’s fastest CPU that will significantly help equip Android mobile devices to handle the complexities of multi-modal AI.

The macro trend of AI-powered smartphones in the next few years will act as a bullish driver for rising average selling price and will play an important role in Qualcomm’s future growth.

Cristiano Amon, the president and CEO of Qualcomm, has commented on the development of AI powered phones in the Q2 earnings call: “And where we are in the cycle right now, it's kind of similar to what we saw in the feature phone to smartphone transition. You're in the part of the cycle that most of the new features come from the OEM and from the OS provider on AI, and then eventually they are going to start coming from third-party developers, and that’s where it gets scale.”

This article touches on some of the current and forecasted expectations of Qualcomm. However, much of this article will concentrate on the fundamentals of Qualcomm to see the historical performance and to try and determine if this same pattern is likely to continue in the future. I will examine the value vs. the current share price to see if the stock is selling at a bargain price. Finally, I'll let you know if I have intentions of investing in the company.

Snapshot of the Company

The BTMA Stock Analyzer’s company rating score. This score helps to determine the overall health of the company. Qualcomm's score is 77.6 out of 100. Therefore, QCOM seems to have above average fundamentals since most categories produce good scores.

Below, we will start to delve into more details of each category.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer)

Fundamentals

Stock price history is the first topic that I would like to focus on. Below in the chart, we can see that stock price has been increasing over the past 10 years. However, it has been a somewhat bumpy ride, and the price movement has many expected frequent climbs and falls. The stock saw limited price growth in the first four years of the chart, from 2015 to 2019. But from 2019 to 2025, there was accelerated growth. Overall, the stock has experienced an annual growth rate of 10.53% during the past decade.

Earnings

While focusing on earnings history, we can see that earnings have been somewhat volatile. In fact, 2018 even saw negative earnings, which were attributed to various factors (Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, a penalty by the European Commission, a patent dispute with Apple (AAPL), and the termination of NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) acquisition). Despite this outlier year, it’s worth noting that the earnings have been climbing overall during the past 10 years.

Consistent earnings can help us to more easier to estimate future growth and stock value. But QCOM’s volatile earnings make it a bit more difficult to accurately estimate future growth and current value.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – EPS History)

Besides stock price history and earnings, it’s also important to examine other metrics such as gross margins, return on invested capital, and return on equity.

Return on Equity

The return on equity (ROE) has remained strong over the past five years. However, the significant drop in ROE in the past two years is worrisome and should be cautiously watched moving forward to make sure that a downward trend doesn’t continue. I prefer to invest in stocks with a five-year average of 16% or more. Hence, QCOM easily meets my requirements.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – ROE History)

Next, we'll compare the return on equity of this company to its industry. The average return on equity of 30 Semiconductor Equipment companies is 31.78%.

So, QCOM’s 5-year average of 75.78% is well above average, and current ROE of 42.39 is also above average.

Return on Invested Capital

The return on invested capital peaked in 2022 at 44.88 and dropped significantly in 2023 and 2024. ROIC fell nearly 23% during the period, but five-year average ROIC is above expectations at around 26.1%. My preference for Return on Invested Capital is a five-year average of 16% or more. So, QCOM beats this mark as well.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Return on Invested Capital History)

Gross Margin Percent

Qualcomm's gross margin percent (GMP) has slightly decreased over the past five years but is still at very high levels. The five-year average GMP stands at 57.58%, which shows consistent profitability. My preference is for companies with gross margin percent (GMP) consistently above 30%. QCOM has maintained acceptable margins over a long period; however, this deterioration of gross margin should be monitored moving onward.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Gross Margin Percent History)

Financial Stability

When reviewing Qualcomm's balance sheet, we can see that the debt-to-equity is less than 1. This is what we want. It tells us that Qualcomm owns more than it owes. It's a good sign of financial stability.

Qualcomm also has a satisfactory Current Ratio of 2.73. This indicates that it has an adequate ability to use its assets to pay its short-term debt.

Normally, I want to see a Current Ratio of more than 1, and QCOM exceeds that level, which makes the stock less prone to limited short-term liquidity.

In terms of the balance sheet, Qualcomm seems to be in good financial health. In the short term the company is generating enough cash flow to cover its obligations and in the long term the company has more equity than debt.

QCOM currently pays a dividend, with a dividend yield at 2.02%.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Misc. Fundamentals)

The next step is to try and estimate the value of the company and to see if it might be selling at a bargain price.

Value Vs. Price

Qualcomm’s Price-Earnings Ratio of 16.27 suggests that QCOM could be selling at a high price when comparing QCOM’s PE Ratio to the long-term S&P 500 market average PE Ratio of 15. But since Qualcomm is considered a tech growth company, it's typical PE ratio is often higher than the market average PE Ratio.

The 10-year and five-year average PE Ratio of QCOM has typically been 21.8 and 20.3, respectively. This suggests that QCOM could actually be trading at a low price when compared to its average historical PE Ratio range.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Stock Value)

The estimated value of the stock is $164.05, compared to the current stock price of $154.29. This suggests that QCOM is now selling at a bargain price.

Next I'll do a more detailed valuation analysis. For this analysis, I will use a diluted EPS of 8.97. This valuation calculates growth and value based on earnings, book value, and total equity. It also considers low and average growth rates.

The table below displays the various growth rates over the past 10 years and five years. Below the table, in the chart, the vertical valuation lines correspond to the table valuation ranges. The dots on the lines represent current stock price. If a dot is near the bottom of the valuation range line, this would suggest that the stock is undervalued. If a dot is toward the top of the valuation line, this would indicate an overvalued stock.

(Source: BTMA Wealth Builders Club)

My detailed analysis gives us an average valuation of approximately $168 per share compared to its current price of about $154. This suggests that Qualcomm is slightly undervalued. However, the valuation based on low to average forward growth is between $117 and $123.

Summarizing the Fundamentals

Based on the facts, Qualcomm is a financially healthy company in the long term with a debt-to-equity ratio under 1. For the short term, its current ratio of 2.73 tells us that Qualcomm has enough cash to cover its short-term liabilities.

This company has 10 years of earnings growth with some volatility and one year of negative earnings.

Fundamentals such as ROIC, gross margins, and ROE are at excellent levels. However, all of the above-mentioned fundamentals have been on a declining trend and should be cautiously monitored.

Another consideration is Qualcomm’s expanding leadership in AI and automotive chip technologies and future AI-enabled smartphones. Qualcomm is well positioned to maintain strong performance and unlock new growth opportunities in the upcoming years.

Regarding valuation, my analysis shows that the stock is slightly undervalued.

Qualcomm Vs. The S&P 500

Next, we'll see how Qualcomm compares to the S&P 500 US stock market benchmark during the past 10 years. In the chart below, notice that QCOM has mostly underperformed the S&P 500 over the past decade. But for the patient investor, Qualcomm’s volatility has enabled the stock to briefly outperform the S&P 500 during peak moments. Therefore, if you're able to buy QCOM at a significant discount and are fortunate enough to sell during a peak, you could end up with a nice profit, but this is easier said than done for most investors.

Forward-Looking Conclusion

Qualcomm has been taking on more efforts to diversify the business between segments of smartphone, Internet of Things (IoT), and AI-enabled personal computers. This should help the company diversify while at the same time mitigate some risks moving forward. This is especially important when moving forward because of the company's heavy focus and dependency on smartphones and its dealings with Apple. Since Apple is developing modem chips of its own, this could significantly decrease Qualcomm's revenue from one of its biggest clients. Therefore, I believe that Qualcomm is making a wise decision to diversify more into other business segments in order to continue growth into the future.

Considering Qualcomm's new developments and diversification, the analysts are expecting Qualcomm's future earnings growth to occur at an average annual rate of 6.49% over the next five years.

Additionally, the average one-year price target for this stock is at $171.47. This is about a 7.78% increase in a year.

The expected annual compounding rate of return is 10.99%.

Considering analysts’ forecasts, you might expect about 6% (low growth) to 11% (average growth) annually.

QCOM’s past earnings growth shows us that during the past 10 and 5-year periods, the average EPS growth rate was about 11.8% and 16.8%, respectively.

However, when focusing on cash flow growth over the past ten and five years, the average growth has been 6.4% and 14.9%, respectively. In summary, when calculating all of these return possibilities, the average annual return could likely be in the range of 9%-10%.

Comparatively, the S&P 500’s average return from 1928 to present time is around 10%. Therefore, Qualcomm's possible return of 9%-10% would only return as much as or less than an S&P 500 index fund. The individual QCOM stock obviously doesn’t offer the diversification that the index fund does, and it fails to outperform the index fund over the long haul.

Does Qualcomm Pass My Checklist?

  1. Company Rating 70+ out of 100? YES (77.625)
  2. Share Price Compound Annual Growth Rate > 12%? NO (10.53%)
  3. Earnings history mostly increasing? NO
  4. ROE (5-year average 16% or greater)? YES (75%)
  5. ROIC (5-year average 16% or greater)? YES (31%)
  6. Gross Margin % (5-year average > 30%)? YES (57%)
  7. Debt-to-Equity (less than 1)? YES
  8. Current Ratio (greater than 1)? YES (2.73)
  9. Outperformed S&P 500 during most of the past 10 years? NO
  10. In my opinion, will this company will continue to successfully sell their same main product/service for the next 10 years? YES (however, I could see significant modifications to its current products)

Qualcomm scored 7/10 or 70%. Therefore, QCOM could be worth considering as a potential investment.

Is Qualcomm currently selling at a bargain price?

  1. Estimated Value greater than Current Stock Price? YES (Value $164.05 vs $154 Stock Price).
  2. Detailed Valuation greater than Current Stock Price? YES (Value $168 vs $154 Stock Price).

Valuation metrics suggest that Qualcomm might be selling at a bargain price. But analysts’ lowered forecasted growth indicates that the stock could fall to lower valuations of around $130 or less.

Overall, I feel that Qualcomm is a solid company that has had good fundamental performance. However, I'm concerned about its deteriorating return on equity, return on invested capital, and declining gross margins.

Another concern is the lowered forecasted growth. It seems that at best, the stock is only slightly undervalued and, in my view, there isn’t enough upside potential to justify investing in QCOM at this time.

I understand that I have better opportunities elsewhere to invest my money for more upside potential. Even a long-term investment in the S&P 500 could provide safer, more diversified, and better long-term returns than an investment in QCOM at its current price.

data.title: Bank7 Corp. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Bank7 Corp. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: OFG Bancorp: Strong Performer On Most Levels
data.description:
Today, we continue our regional bank coverage with the just-reported earnings out of OFG Bancorp (NYSE:OFG). For those unfamiliar with this regional bank, OFG Bancorp is the parent/holding company for Oriental Bank. Now, despite what the name may imply, this bank does business in a special region of U.S. territories. The bank completely operates under U.S., Puerto Rico, and U.S. Virgin Islands banking laws and regulations. As you might expect, the bank provides consumer and commercial banking, lending, and wealth management products, services, and technology. The bank does business primarily in Puerto Rico and U.S. Virgin Islands. Despite the economic pressure Puerto Rico has been under, which has been recovering from one hurricane after another, the bank is in growth mode. At today's levels, we rate the bank a hold, but a market selloff would be an opportunity to get long this quality operator.

OFG Bancorp's Q2 Headline Performance

The Q2 report from OFG Bancorp saw a revenue and earnings beat versus expectations. With Q2 revenues of $182.2 million, the bank saw a 1.6% increase year-over-year in this key metric. This was a slight beat versus consensus by $0.52 million. On the back of expense control and well-managed provisions for credit losses, as well as a strong net interest margin, earnings were beat. Margins are well over 5% with this bank, but are volatile. Net income hit $51.8 million, or $1.15 per share. This was a $0.10 beat versus estimates, rising $0.7 million year-over-year and $6 million from the sequential Q1.

Share price relative to book value?

When looking to purchase equity in a bank stock, we need to look at price relative to book value. The bank's stock is just 2 points off yearly highs at $45. What about relative to the book value per share reported in Q2? Book value per share was $29.83, so we have a huge premium-to-book here.

However, we really like to compare the equity relative to the tangible book. Tangible book was $27.67. Getting bank stocks at tangible book is almost always a buy, but it is hard to do, barring a massive pullback. But the premium-to-tangible book is pretty steep here, despite solid performance. We think it justifies a hold and waiting for a sizable retracement in the market.

OFG Bancorp's loans and deposits both grow sequentially

We always examine movement in loans and deposits, as these are key metrics for any bank we analyze. Loans at the end of Q2 were $8.18 billion, and this was an increase from the sequential Q1 of about $328 million, but is up 7.1% from a year ago. This is a big positive. What about deposits? Well, deposits were up to $9.90 billion, an increase of $139 million from $9.76 billion in the sequential quarter, and up $291.0 million from $9.60 billion a year ago. There were higher savings deposit balances, higher commercial and government balances, but slightly lower retail balances.

One slight weakness was that the net interest margin dipped 11 basis points from the sequential quarter but is still a very strong 5.31%. This is among the highest-margin regional banks we cover.

OFG Bancorp's asset quality and return metrics

There is strong asset quality here. In Q2, there were $21.7 million of loan loss provisions, down $4 million compared to $25.7 million in Q1. This was a result of $17.2 million for increased loan volume, $3.7 million in specific reserves on four commercial loans, and $0.7 million for specific Puerto Rico risk factors. But the decline helped EPS.

The allowance for credit losses on loans was $189 million, or 2.32% of total loans. This is up very slightly compared with $181 million in Q1, or 2.31% of loans. Nothing to be concerned with in the pattern there, though over 2% is a bit higher than average compared to most regional we cover. Net charge-offs are minimal. They were just $12.8 million, down from $20.4 million in Q1, and down from $15.0 million a year ago. Nonperforming loans were up to 1.21% of all loans, 8 basis points higher than 1.13%.

Given this asset quality and the valuation, we still like a hold here and are waiting for a retracement. This comes as the return metrics improved from Q1.

Finally, the bank is pretty efficient, right around the ideal 50% mark we look for. The efficiency ratio hit 52.04%, improving 40 basis points from the sequential quarter. Despite a slightly lower margin, the returns on average assets and equity both improved, hitting 1.73% and 16.96%, respectively. These were improvements of 17 basis points and 168 basis points, respectively.

Forward View

All things considered, we think OFG Bancorp stock is fully valued here. There is a big premium-to-book and premium-to-tangible book. On the plus side, margins are strong and EPS is growing. However, asset quality is a bit mixed, with a higher-than-average allowance for credit losses and nonperforming loans than most regionals we cover. Still, the return metrics are strong. This is a fine operator, but we think new money should hold until the next inevitable market drawdown to get a better entry.

data.title: Noise And Opportunity In Emerging Markets Debt
data.description:
In January, our emerging markets (EM) debt outlook called for steady growth, manageable inflation, and resilience in the face of geopolitical noise. Six months in, how has that view held up? From macro dynamics to credit spreads, trade policy to local market flows, much of what we anticipated is playing out—but with a few important surprises along the way.

When you released your EM outlook in January, you believed EM gross domestic product (GDP) growth would remain stable in 2025. How has that played out?

As we expected, EM macro dynamics remained resilient. Overall, expectations were for deceleration of the global economy, predominantly driven by the impact that rising U.S. tariffs could have on global trade. However, we are seeing just a marginal repricing of expectations for EM growth, and we believe that growth should remain between 3.5% and 4.0% going forward, which highlights the resilience of EM economies.

How did your inflation expectations play out?

Overall, pretty much as we expected. Inflation has generally been benign across the board, with few exceptions. This has allowed most EM central banks to decouple from the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) and continue cutting interest rates, providing an additional stimulus for growth.

In your opinion, what is the main reason for this resilience?

The main reason—in addition to monetary stimulus and benign inflationary dynamics—is that EMs have reduced dependence on the U.S. economy over the years, and increased trade between themselves. So at this point many EM economies are somewhat isolated from what’s happening in the United States.

You also noted in January that the Trump presidency introduced uncertainty due to his positions on immigration, taxation, trade, regulation, and energy. Has the reality differed from the expectation?

The situation has unfolded as we expected. We never believed that the changes would create a major disruption in the global economy. We believed that there would be more bark than bite, or escalating to de-escalate, especially about trade policy.

This is exactly what we’re seeing. There was a lot of noise after “Liberation Day,” followed by a more pragmatic approach from the U.S. administration, which opened channels for dialogue and negotiation. A good example was how the United States and China backed off from very high tariffs, postponing absurd levels as they engaged in conversations.

But there are still open questions. Over the next few months, we expect trade agreements reached with important U.S. trading partners. Now, as then, we believe investors need to separate noise from fact.

We saw many markets struggle after the “Liberation Day” announcement. How did EM debt fare in that environment?

There was a market correction after “Liberation Day” because investors were concerned about the potential impact of U.S. trade policy on the global economy. I think the markets were starting to price in a global recession, and pretty much all asset classes sold off. Then, as the U.S. government started signaling that it was open to negotiation with trade partners, the markets calmed.

How did your portfolios fare in the wake of the tariff announcements? Did you make any adjustments?

We were expecting some noise when the new U.S. administration took over, so we decided to reduce risks in our portfolios across the board by increasing cash and reducing overall beta. Then, as markets sold off and valuations became more attractive, we started to rebuild the positions and increase risk exposure. When investors became less concerned about the potential implications for the global economy, and markets started to rally again, our portfolios benefited.

Could you give some examples of EM countries that have both benefited and been hurt by U.S. tariff changes?

It’s too early to say which countries will benefit and which countries will not, because the U.S. government is in the middle of negotiations with several important trading partners, and we just don’t know where things will land. But I think it’s safe to say that countries that compete with the United States could benefit if the United States enters a trade war with Asia (and China more specifically). For example, if China buys fewer agricultural products from the United States, it will have to buy more from South America, and important producers of agricultural commodities such as Brazil and Argentina could benefit.

You spoke a bit about the oil outlook in January; could you provide an update?

We were anticipating lower oil prices due to rising production from OPEC+ and softening demand due to a deceleration in global growth. Rising tensions in the Middle East drove oil prices significantly higher. Now we’re seeing oil prices fall again. We believe oil prices will move marginally lower from here due to higher supply, but we don’t expect prices to move abruptly because we don’t believe the global economy will enter a recession. We believe the moving prices are just an accommodation in terms of growth dynamics, which in turn should support the demand for energy.

Where do you see opportunities in hard currency EM debt?

At the start of the year, our hard currency strategy emphasized an overweight to high-yield debt and an underweight to investment-grade credit, based on the view that high yield offered better value. We expected spread compression between the two segments, and that dynamic has played out, benefiting our portfolios. We continue to prefer high-yield debt and remain positioned for further compression.

Beyond credit quality, we see opportunities in countries with access to multilateral and bilateral financing, those resilient to trade disruptions, and those that could even benefit from rising trade tensions. Frontier markets and corporate credit also stand out, particularly given the breadth and diversity of the opportunity set.

More broadly, the environment for EM hard currency debt remains constructive. Yields and carry are high, default risk appears low (we don’t expect any significant credit events over the next 18 to 24 months), and fundamentals are holding up. Technical conditions are also favorable, with net new issuance expected to stay limited. Altogether, we believe it’s a solid backdrop for the asset class.

Where do you see opportunities in local currency EM debt?

Local currency EM debt is particularly compelling right now, given our view that the U.S. dollar will continue to depreciate. Much local market debt offers higher nominal and real interest rates than the United States and other advanced economies, as well as undervalued currencies.

Are there any new developments in frontier EMD?

From a hard currency perspective, default risk is low, and frontier countries still offer decent value in terms of credit spreads. But we’re mostly excited about local currency debt in frontier markets.

More and more frontier countries have been opening to international investors, so the opportunity set keeps expanding. This allows us to invest in a very diversified way.

Plus, with the U.S. dollar weaker, these countries can better manage their currencies. In most frontier markets, we see high local interest rates and stable to appreciating currencies because of the weaker dollar.

So, it’s a very interesting place to be right now—an asset class that we believe is misunderstood, mispriced, and where the expected potential returns, in our opinion, outweigh the risks.

Where are you seeing the most compelling opportunities in EM corporate credit right now, and what does recent issuance suggest about investor appetite and refinancing risk?

We believe EM corporate credit continues to offer attractive risk-reward opportunities, supported by a more stable macro backdrop, easing rate volatility, and still-low default rates.

While EM corporates have lagged longer-duration credit this year, we see room for catch-up, particularly in high-yield segments with manageable leverage.

Regionally, opportunities span across geographies. In Latin America, Brazilian metals, oil-linked names, and segments of the bank capital structure remain appealing. In Central and Eastern Europe, Middle East, and Africa (CEEMEA), Turkish corporates offer high yields, and Middle Eastern demand has proven resilient. In Asia, Indian and Indonesian utilities stand out, while China remains highly idiosyncratic.

Meanwhile, the primary market has stayed healthy in 2025. Issuance briefly slowed in April but rebounded strongly in May and June, signaling solid investor appetite. Gross supply is now expected to surpass earlier projections, even as net financing stays negative. Refinancing accounts for about one-third of issuance, which is in line with historical norms, and we see no signs of stress. Latin America and the Middle East continue to lead supply, with Asia rebounding but still below pre-COVID levels.

Are you watching any potential developments for the remainder of 2025?

We believe the asset class is influenced by two predominant forces: global growth and global liquidity. So, we need to analyze the risk factors that will impact this balance.

The first risk factor is trade-related—any tensions or trade wars that could significantly impact global trade, resulting in weaker economic growth and even a recessionary environment. The key is to watch how trade negotiations unfold.

The second risk factor is inflation—that is, whether a significant adjustment in U.S. tariffs leads to higher U.S. inflation and forces the Fed to tighten monetary conditions. We think that’s unlikely because we expect a pragmatic approach from the U.S. government, but it’s a risk.

If we see a combination of weaker growth, rising inflation, and tighter monetary conditions, we expect that risk assets, including EM debt, will likely be affected.

That said, we believe risks are low in terms of growth and inflation. Although there has been some increase in U.S. tariffs, so far we have not seen any significant impacts on growth and inflation. So, I believe investors were overly concerned about these risks in the beginning of the year, and today there is more confidence that we will muddle through this situation.

Can you sum up what’s different now versus January in a few sentences?

I think there is less uncertainty. In the beginning of the year, we didn’t know what to expect in terms of U.S. policies. Today, there is less uncertainty about the policy agenda and also more confidence that there will be pragmatism. And in our opinion, that’s good news for investors.

Marcelo Assalin, CFA, partner, is the head of William Blair’s emerging markets debt team, on which he also serves as a portfolio manager.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Caribou Biosciences: Key Data Updates Coming, But I'm Concerned About Near-Term Volatility
data.description:
Topline Summary

Caribou Biosciences, Inc. (NASDAQ:CRBU) is a cell therapy-focused biotech working to commercialize gene-edited, allogeneic CAR T-cell treatments for different forms of blood cancer. As they have surged in value over the past few months, let's take a look at their current pipeline, what factors have driven their rise since May, and whether things are likely to continue in a positive direction.

Pipeline Overview

CRBU is currently developing a pair of CAR T-cell therapies in the clinic. These have different targets and some different edits to make them work in the unique clinical scenarios they're being explored in. But both are made using CRISPR-based gene editing. Let's take a closer look.

CB-010

The first CAR T-cell project in development is CB-010, which is directed toward CD19 (the same target as the multiple approved CAR T-cell therapies like Kymriah). CRBU hopes that by employing allogeneic T cells in the modification, that healthier T cells can be used without the added time to have patients with cancer undergo apheresis and then modify the cells.

CB-010 also employs genomic "knock-outs" of the T cell receptor and PD-1, eliminating their expression in the final product to improve antigen recognition and prevent suppression of activity by the patient's immune system. The knockout of the T cell receptor is also hoped to reduce the risk of graft versus host disease, a common and frightening complication associated with bone marrow allografts that are in use for different blood cancers like leukemia.

Since CD19 is a lineage marker specific to B cells, it makes sense that CB-010 is being explored primarily in the setting of B cell malignancies, such as non-Hodgkin lymphoma. The key clinical trial in this setting is ANTLER, with findings from the dose escalation portion being presented last year at the ASCO meeting.

Most notably, of the 4 patients in the study with large B cell lymphomas (such as DLBCL), 100% had an ongoing response lasting over a year. This has prompted the company to open a dose expansion cohort of ANTLER enrolling patients with large B cell lymphomas, and the median progression-free survival looked particularly promising in patients who had higher levels of HLA matching (typically used to determine compatibility of bone marrow donors and patients).

Moreover, patients treated with CB-010 appeared to have reduced risk of key adverse events compared (across trials, not head-to-head) with a trial of Yescarta. In particular, neurologic complications and cytokine release syndrome appeared to be less common with CB-010 treatment, and no patients have experienced graft versus host disease to date.

In this larger cohort of 40 patients with large B-cell lymphoma, the overall response rate was 73%, many of these responses being complete (i.e., no evidence of tumor).

Since ASCO 2024, we have not heard much from this program, but CRBU is guiding for a "robust" update in the second half of 2025.

CB-011

The other program for CRBU is focused on exploiting the B-cell maturation antigen (BCMA), an important marker of plasma cells that remains in place in patients who develop the plasma cell dyscrasia multiple myeloma. This cell surface marker is the subject of substantial research and commercial interest, with the approval of autologous CAR T-cell therapies and other immunotherapeutic approaches being available today.

CB-011 hopes to build on this approach, enrolling patients into the phase 1 CaMMouflage trial to evaluate the safety and preliminary efficacy in the setting of relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma. To date, we have not seen a readout of this study, but CRBU intends to present data in the second half of 2025.

Financial Overview

At the end of Q1 2025, CRBU held $29.4 million in cash and equivalents, as well as another $179.0 million in short-term marketable securities. They had another $13.2 million in non-current investments. They recognized $2.4 million in license and collaboration revenue set against $45.3 million in operating expenses. After recognition of other income, the net loss for the quarter was $40 million.

Negative cash flow for the quarter was $36.7 million, implying a cash runway of approximately 6 quarters, considering the non-current liquid assets they have at their disposal. This would imply a cash runway taking CRBU into the second half of 2026, but their recent prioritization and workforce optimization efforts should reduce costs, and CRBU is guiding that they should remain operational in the second half of 2027.

Strengths and Risks

Strength - Unique, still-untapped approach to engineered cell therapy

Although the targets of interest are anything but unique (there is a wide range of approved cell therapies targeting CD19 or BCMA), the allogeneic approach has the opportunity to address real manufacturing and clinical bottlenecks that currently limit the use of these approaches. If CRBU can demonstrate at least plausible activity on par with autologous cell therapies, then it is likely the FDA will be comfortable moving forward with an approval in the future, in my opinion.

Couple that with early signals that complications like CRS and ICANS are less common with CRB-010, and you have the opportunity to demonstrate best-in-class potential. All of that is very exciting, as CD19 and BCMA-directed CAR T-cell therapies are to date the only ones that have shown feasibility as a commercial product (Adaptimmune's and Iovance's struggles, for example, showcase the fact that an FDA approval is not, in itself, the end of the road to success).

Risk - HLA matching seems to be needed?

There is one wrinkle at play, though, with the data we've seen for CRB-010, and that is the apparent need for HLA matching with the cell therapy. Obviously, in a patient who is donating their own T cells for the therapy, matching is a non-issue. But the fact that efficacy with CRB-010 appears to be almost restricted to those patients who have at least 4 HLA matches to the donor cell therapy suggests that rejection by the patient is a serious obstacle.

The requirements are not as robust as in something like allogeneic bone marrow transplant, where you're looking for matching of almost all the HLA genes (between 8 and 12 possible matches, depending on the level of resolution you have) to avoid graft rejection and GVHD. This is changing, of course, but when you start venturing into "unmatched" on any level, the efforts to prevent GVHD start to get more intense.

The need for HLA matching may end up severely restricting the potential market size for these cell therapies, and it's an issue worth paying close attention to.

Risk - Cash position is strengthened with the reorganization, but we don't know what it is exactly

We do have an idea of how much CRBU anticipates saving with their workforce reductions and prioritization of clinical development, but their extension of the cash runway by a full year may very well be too optimistic. At any rate, given that CRBU remains in a phase 1 study at this time, it is reasonable to predict that the company will need to take on an equity raise to fund operations for commercialization. And it is not possible to know at this time whether that would be from a position of strength.

Bottom Line Summary

As I write this, CRBU has surged from a market cap of around $61 million (share price $0.66) to $170 million (share price $1.83) since the middle of May. In my view, this wasn't really driven by any specific clinical catalyst, as we have not had a formal data update from ANTLER since the ASCO 2024 timeframe.

More likely, this is due to improving market sentiment from the reprioritization efforts and the anticipation of critical data readouts in the back half of the year. For me, this rapid ascent on the back of "anticipation" rather than genuine news suggests that people are loading up with the expectation of making a quick trade.

This spells substantial volatility, with the strong risk of a decline in share price even if (perhaps especially if) the news happens to be good. And anyone buying in today will face a long wait to commercialization, with anything but a guarantee that the market will appreciate the results and potential here.

Count me a fan of CRBU as a science project. But at this time I feel there is likely to be too much volatility to be worth jumping in as an investment. The clinical data need to continue to mature. For that reason, I view this current price point as a "Hold." They are trading below their total liquidity, but that won't prevent a further decline in share price, especially since the valuation has risen so much over the past few months.

data.title: Magna International: More Grit Than Grip In The Financials - Sell
data.description:
Intro

Brief history: Magna International (TSX:MG:CA) (NYSE:MGA) was founded in 1957 by Frank Stronach in Toronto, Canada, and began as Multimatic Investments Ltd. to supply automotive parts, starting with a contract for General Motors. In the following decade, the company had eight operating plants. The IPO took place in 1969 after a merger with Magna Electronics Corporation (aerospace, defence and industrial components company). The merged entity became known as Magna International in 1973.

The company has grown into North America’s largest automotive parts manufacturer and Tier-1 supplier (#3 worldwide after privately-held Bosch and ZF Friedrichshafen). Magna achieved milestones like acquiring German gear-box / transmission producer Getrag for 1.9 billion USD in 2015, developing rearview cameras for Hummers in 2005, and more recently partnering with NVIDIA for advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS).

Magna is known and respected for its innovative mobility solutions. The company not only produces components or car bodies (e.g. for the Mercedes-Benz GLE), but also assembles entire cars itself for third-parties, like the lower-volume, but prestigious Mercedes-Benz G-Class or BMW Z4.

The stock has recovered from its April-dip, climbing from 30 bucks to 42 USD as of writing. However, and despite low-looking valuation multiples, I think the road might be bumpy from here. Long-term fundamentals have been in reverse gear, while being masked by a modest sales increase. I rate Magna a "sell" because the upside seems rather limited, with weak fundamentals not offering a decent-enough margin of safety.

Magna-ficent times likely over

As a supplier, Magna rises and falls with worldwide OEM (original equipment manufacturer) vehicle production. That should not be really surprising.

Since 2000, car production has been in a long-term bull market, according to Statista. Due to the cyclical nature of cars, being discretionary purchases, from time to time, production numbers fall. But historically, after a few years, a recovery has started and brought new highs. The following chart reaches into 2023. It includes three recessionary periods.

Here is the same chart showing the production number for 2023 in the box. Despite the strong comeback from the 2020-low, the former highs from 2017–2018 have not been taken out, yet.

It is not only interesting that the old highs have not been reached, but that the highs occurred a couple of years before the big smash came.

That said, the year 2024 is missing on the charts above. Magna is so kind to supply the missing number with their annual report 2024. The figure was 89 million – not only far off the 97 million record from 2017–2018, but also lower compared to 2023, signaling the recent recovery could be already over.

With total volumes being down year over year, not having seen a new high for years (almost a decade), and no signs this is about to change for the time being, my bearish thesis started to build torque. When car volumes fall, it is unlikely that Magna will see a strong development against the overall trend.

Having a look at sales below, we could draw the conclusion that Magna is competing quite well in this challenging environment. At least it is not knee-deep in trouble like the world's No. 2 ZF Friedrichshafen, which posted high losses and big layoffs.

Long-term, Magna's sales are on the rise, having been only temporarily paused in between. Despite lower total car volumes in 2024 compared to 2023 (and significantly lower vs. 2017–2018), Magna posted minimally better sales than in 2023. The 42.8 billion USD were a new sales record.

Unfortunately, the good news end here. Taking now a look at the margin development, we must acknowledge they have been going in the wrong direction for quite some time.

While the drop in gross margins was comparatively mild (from 14.2% to 13.6% per last count), operating margins as displayed by the green line below, have cratered by a third over the last ten years. For a low-margin business with high capital-intensity and high fixed costs, this is a hefty development.

Likewise noteworthy, operating margins have not risen with sales over the last years, making the sales records appearing in a totally different light.

In consequence, operating earnings were not able to achieve new highs. I should add, they were not even able to reach the former levels pre-2020, despite a higher sales base.

Unfortunately, interest expenses have risen quite a lot ...

… and have left a massive dent on the bottom line.

In times of record-high sales (per year-end 2024), this might be less of an issue. But what if less sunny times come?

The first quarter 2025, despite having been celebrated as "above expectations", was not really convincing. Sales were down by a hefty 8% as global car production fell by 3%. While China was a bright spot with +2%, North America (–5%) and Europe (–8%) were a drag on results.

The biggest segment by sales, but unfortunately also the one with the highest operating margins, Body Exteriors & Structures, performed under-whelmingly. There's also the troubled Seating & Systems segment with a negative impression, but it is comparatively small, contributing even less to to the whole. Nonetheless, a loss is not pleasing.

Digging a bit deeper into the numbers, my overall impression only got confirmed. Magna has been losing efficiency for years.

For example, due to lower profitability, returns on assets, capital and equity have been falling.

At the same time, inventory turnover has been falling (less dynamic) while average days inventory outstanding have been on the rise, signaling a slow-down in efficiency and working capital management.

Days payables outstanding have risen (time until Magna pays its suppliers) while cash conversion takes longer than before. Magna seems to be financing itself through pressuring its own suppliers, at least partially. This is not uncommon in the auto industry that the bigger flexes their muscles, but this cannot be played forever without at some point putting suppliers on the verge of bankruptcy.

While the balance sheet is okay-ish as a whole, certain liquidity ratios have worsened – in what was a bull market for the auto industry.

Nothing extremely dangerous, as current liabilities are fully covered by current assets. But I like to have a look at long-term trends to get a feeling for the dynamics.

Speaking of the balance sheet, Magna per last count had a billion USD in cash. Cash flow generation is positive so that the maturing debt pieces should not be a big problem to service.

Where I am concerned about is the economic situation and the tariff issues. Magna is a victim of import tariffs, but big parts should be exempt. It remains to be seen, as the playing rules could change practically in short notice. But it is a potential risk.

The outlook looks quite reasonable – at first sight. The previous weak sales guidance foresaw a noticeable decline, but it has been upped. However, only as a result of stronger currencies against the USD (in which Magna is reporting, despite being a Canadian company). This could change quickly if the USD starts to rebound, making this a dangerous adventure to rely on currency movements.

Effectively and organically, sales will likely not rise. With weak volumes this will be close to impossible, as the industry is facing cost pressure. Furthermore, the expected adj. EBIT margin was lowered. As discussed above, profitability has been on the decline for quite some time.

Despite Magna having been profitable in every year over the last rolling decade, I wanted to remind readers what can happen if the economy turns down sharply.

Maybe everything bad is already priced in?

Valuation

It does not look like that. On an enterprise value to sales basis, unfortunately only back ten years, Magna look to be in no-man's land, respectively maybe a bit below the average for this period. 2021 was clearly an exaggeration, but else the range was between 0.4–0.6x EV / sales. Currently, stock trades at 0.5x. Dips can lead to figures below 0.4x.

Important to keep in mind, that we are using last year's record-high sales figure as basis. Should sales fall, the valuation will look entirely different.

Price to tangible book looks a bit friendlier – when comparing to historical averages. I think this is a dangerous approach. A figure above 1x TBV for the auto industry is generally considered to be rather a valuation premium. No matter the average.

Mercedes-Benz for example with higher profitably and being in a stronger bargaining position is trading currently only at 0.7x TBV. Magna trades for twice that. Magna in the past had a premium to Mercedes-Benz, for whatever reason.

But there are times when this gap narrows or even closes. And this should happen again, so either Mercedes is much too cheap or, what I believe, Magna could tailspin.

In that sense, Magna does not look to be cheap enough. With business momentum pointing down, there is not reason to hurry. To the contrary, I am seeing to many risks, making the stock a "sell" for me.

Risks to my Thesis

If the economy turns and automotive demand recovers, Magna could see a dramatic improvement in its fundamentals.

Cost cutting measures could save margins.

A recession could be mild, not pressuring the stock much.

If Magna, which is a complex entity, decides to sell or spin-off for example its problematic seating business, this could unlock shareholder value, sending the stock higher.

Conclusion

Magna looks modestly attractive on the surface. But under the hood, financials have ben deteriorating for years. Higher and record sales did not lead to strong profitability, to the contrary. With industry momentum having turned down, there is little reason to own Magna stock now. Sell.

data.title: Bitwise Bitcoin ETF: Bull Market Still Running On One Engine
data.description:
It's been ~18 months since the spot Bitcoin ETFs began trading, and at this juncture, most investors have moved beyond the granular details in their preference for a specific ETF to gain direct exposure to Bitcoin (BTC-USD). The ETFs all offer similar structure and tracking accuracy, save for Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (GBTC), which has maintained a higher 1.5% expense ratio. We're also now past the early marketing incentives, like the six-month fee waiver on some of the ETFs.

Personally, I favor the Bitwise Bitcoin ETF (NYSEARCA:BITB) as my preferred vehicle because of the fund’s alignment with the broader Bitcoin development ethos; they donate 10% of their profits to open-source Bitcoin development. Bitwise was also the first fund manager to publish the Bitcoin addresses for its ETF holdings. Investors can track the inflows and outflows of the actual BTC in the fund in real-time, using the addresses.

This week, Bitwise retired the approach of verifying the ETF's BTC holdings via the published addresses and has introduced a more robust and institutional-grade framework for verifying the fund’s on-chain reserves and financial integrity through a third-party proof-of-reserves system, verified daily by a U.S.-based licensed CPA firm. Instead of just listing on-chain BTC holdings, this new verification method reconciles on-chain holdings with the ETFs' outstanding shares and will soon include CPA-attested reports that verify both assets and liabilities, thereby delivering a more comprehensive and investor-friendly form of transparency. Think of this in a similar vein to the independent audit performed on companies prior to a 10-Q or 10-K filing, but the scope here is limited to on-chain digital asset balances and ETF share liabilities. And the verification happens daily.

With the latest updates on BITB and their proof-of-reserves verification already mentioned, the truth is, there’s not much left to say about the structure of spot Bitcoin ETFs themselves that hasn't already been covered by analysts here on Seeking Alpha. The products are now largely standardized - same underlying asset, similar tracking, fees narrowing, and with early incentives fading. I believe the questions now on most investors' minds would be whether Bitcoin's momentum sustains, navigating macro headwinds and their potential impact on Bitcoin and its related assets, and the profit-taking strategies at BTC's all-time highs. For most investors, it is now much less about which specific spot Bitcoin ETF to choose and more about how the underlying asset performs and how to position to maximize returns.

With the spot Bitcoin ETFs' underlying asset, Bitcoin, showing momentum lately, the ETFs have naturally followed the price action of Bitcoin. The spot Bitcoin ETFs themselves have been an immense catalyst that has made Bitcoin more mainstream this past year, and recently broke a new all-time high just above $121,000. Since the April Liberation Day tariffs sell-offs when BTC dipped below $80,000, the spot Bitcoin ETFs have added over $6 billion in inflows. The spot ETFs now hold a total of ~1.28 million BTC or about 6% of Bitcoin’s total 21 million supply, bringing total assets under management [AuM] for the total ETFs to a staggering $154 billion (as of July 17, 2025). Since the launch of the spot ETFs, Bitcoin has seen accelerated comebacks after every price dip. Every dip has been followed by an uptrend that breaks the previous all-time high; basically, higher highs and higher lows.

The interest in the spot Bitcoin ETFs has been increasingly institutional - the trade volumes of the spot Bitcoin ETFs and the increasing adoption of Bitcoin treasury strategy among publicly-traded companies prove this institutional interest. The cumulative trade volume since the spot Bitcoin ETF launched now surpasses $1 trillion (shown in the preceding chart). And for the first half of this year, spot Bitcoin ETFs nearly matched about 40% of the net inflows into physically backed gold ETFs. Which is impressive, considering the spot Bitcoin ETFs launched less than two years ago.

Physically-backed gold ETFs recorded $38 billion in inflows in H1, while the physically-backed Bitcoin ETFs (ditto the spot Bitcoin ETFs) have seen $14.83 billion in net inflows so far this year, already flipping its own 2024’s net inflows (see the chart below). As both the gold and the Bitcoin flows data show, digital gold (Bitcoin) is gaining ground and competing strongly with physical gold.

Having highlighted these data points that show Bitcoin’s institutional appeal and structural maturity as a mainstream asset, let's go back to the more pressing question for most retail investors at the moment, who make up a great number of the readership here and for the broader retail cohort: how much longer can BTC’s momentum sustain before retail FOMO kicks in and accelerates price discovery even further?

I believe retail participation has always been pivotal for the peak of Bitcoin market cycles, as retail trades typically make up much of the final wave of market momentum, before an inevitable drawdown or price correction. In the current cycle, the late-stage retail buyers (the so-called “dumb money”) have mostly been sidelined so far (as I’ll show from some data), and this absence of broader retail euphoria suggests that the final leg of the bull run is still ahead. I think it is too early to call a cycle top. In Bitcoin’s cycles, historically, retail FOMO tends to accelerate price discovery dramatically toward the cycle top. Therefore, positioning before this general retail FOMO has always been one of the most effective strategies to capture outsized gains from Bitcoin bull markets.

How Has Retail Been Sidelined in the Current Bitcoin Market Cycle

In the past, the price trend of Bitcoin has closely mirrored the volume trend of retail trades on crypto exchanges. That price/volume relationship has been noticeably weaker since the launch of the spot Bitcoin ETFs. Notice from the chart below how since early 2024 (time of the spot ETFs launch), Bitcoin price has decoupled from retail-driven BTC trade volume on crypto exchanges. And it has been more pronounced lately - as BTC rose above $100k, retail volume took an inverse trajectory (as seen in the chart below).

Meanwhile, the spot ETF flows and volume have been directly influencing Bitcoin spot price. As shown in the next chart, there's a clear visual correlation between periods of sustained positive inflows (green bars) and rising Bitcoin prices, and periods of sustained outflows (red bars) and falling Bitcoin prices.

Now, the essence of these two comparisons in this piece - showing waning retail activity vs BTC price correlation with the spot ETF flows - is to highlight the thesis that the market still has room to run.

The next big question is timing when retail re-enters in full force. I’d say there is no analysis that would definitely pinpoint that timing, but based on historical patterns and market psychology, retail typically piles in late in a bull cycle - once price is already well into price discovery, then the media is flooded with back-to-back headlines of fresh all-time high price (the “Bitcoin is going to $1 million” type frenzy), and anecdotal FOMO is everywhere.

The current sentiment backdrop hasn't shown any of these yet. The spot volumes on crypto exchanges I already mentioned, and Google Trends data for Bitcoin search terms, suggest retail is still sidelined. And further on-chain data shows that the retail trade hasn't been going into altcoins either, as some investors may speculate; Bitcoin dominance still remains elevated above 62%. Even Ethereum's (ETH-USD) surge over $3,000 since Tuesday (July 15) has been primarily linked to institutional flows into the spot Ethereum ETFs. The spot Ethereum ETFs saw record daily net inflows of ~$726 million.

My goal in this piece is to make investors understand that the retail frenzy, which typically marks the later stages of a crypto bull cycle, hasn’t arrived yet (as data shown throughout this article have shown). This means Bitcoin still likely has room to run, especially if this early momentum driven by institutional flows continues and draws more attention from mainstream media, thereby giving retail that psychological green light for an entry. Until then, institutional capital will keep driving the trend. But when retail does finally flood in, history suggests it won’t be a subtle entry; it’ll most likely come with euphoric headlines, parabolic price moves, and a FOMO that feeds on itself. We’re not there yet, and that’s exactly where the opportunity lies.

Investors should bear in mind that the spot Bitcoin ETFs’ share prices are directly impacted by the underlying Bitcoin spot price, and with Bitcoin being an inherently volatile asset, the spot ETF shares also carry significant potential for volatility. In the near term, let’s not also forget the potential for a tariff-driven macro shakeup, as the August 1 deadline for President Trump’s reciprocal trade tariffs draws closer.

I'll close this piece with this simile: the Bitcoin bull market is like a twin-engine jet. At the moment, it’s flying on just one engine, which is institutional flows. The other engine - retail participation - hasn’t powered on yet. But when it does, that’s when things really take off.
